origin: Brightest star in the constellation Lyra.
Queen-shaped constellation of the northern sky.
Icy Galilean moon of Jupiter.
Blue supergiant, foot of Orion.
Red supergiant in the shoulder of Orion.
Largest moon of Neptune, retrograde orbit.
Nearest large spiral galaxy to the Milky Way.
Nearest known star beyond the Sun.
Keel of the ship Argo; southern constellation.
Outermost major moon of Uranus.
Planetary nebula in Aquarius.
Supernova remnant in Taurus (M1).
The central star of the solar system around which the Earth orbits.
The brightest star in the night sky, known as the Dog Star and central to the agricultural calendars of ancient Egypt.
The second-brightest star in the night sky, named after the navigator of Menelaus in Greek mythology.
The closest star system to the Sun, named as the brightest star in the constellation Centaurus.
The brightest star in the constellation Boötes, its name is derived from Greek meaning guardian of the bear.
The brightest star in the constellation Auriga, traditionally representing a small goat carried by a shepherd.
The brightest star in the constellation Canis Minor, its name is derived from the Greek for before the dog.
The name derives from the Arabic term for the end of the river, marking the southern extremity of the constellation Eridanus.
The second brightest star in the constellation Centaurus, derived from an Arabic term meaning ground or floor.
The brightest star in the constellation Aquila, its name is derived from the Arabic phrase for the flying eagle.
The brightest star in the constellation Crux, serving as a primary navigational marker in the Southern Hemisphere.
Known as the eye of the bull in the constellation Taurus, this orange giant was traditionally associated with war and misfortune.
Known as the heart of the Scorpion in Scorpius, this red supergiant is often called the rival of Mars due to its similar reddish hue.
The brightest star in the constellation Virgo, representing the ear of grain held by the maiden.
The brighter of the two Gemini twins in Greek mythology, representing the immortal son of Zeus.
Known as the Mouth of the Fish in the constellation Piscis Austrinus, this star has been observed for millennia by various cultures.
The brightest star in the constellation Cygnus, representing the tail of the swan in Arabic astronomy.
Also known as Beta Crucis, this blue giant is the second-brightest star in the Southern Cross constellation.
The brightest star in the constellation Leo, traditionally representing the heart of the lion.
The second brightest star in the constellation Canis Major, derived from the Arabic phrase for the virgins.
One of the twin stars in the constellation Gemini, named after the mortal brother in Greek mythology.
The star marks the stinger of the constellation Scorpius, its name derived from the Arabic word for the raised tail of a scorpion.
Gamma Crucis is the closest red giant star to the solar system and forms the top of the Southern Cross constellation.
A blue giant star in the constellation Orion, whose name translates from Latin as the female warrior.
The second brightest star in Taurus, its name derives from the Arabic for the one who butts, referring to the bull's horns.
The second-brightest star in the constellation Carina, its name combines Latin and Latinized Greek to mean peaceful waters.
A massive blue supergiant forming the central star of Orion's Belt, its name derived from the Arabic for string of pearls.
Derived from the Arabic Al-Nayyir, meaning the bright one, this star is the primary luminary in the constellation Grus.
An Arabic name meaning the girdle, designating the easternmost star in the belt of Orion.
The brightest star in the constellation Ursa Major, marking the base of the tail of the Great Bear.
The brightest star in the constellation Ursa Major, originating from the Arabic phrase for the back of the Great Bear.
The brightest star in the constellation Perseus, its name is derived from the Arabic phrase for the elbow of the Pleiades.
A bright yellow-white supergiant in the constellation Canis Major, whose name derives from the Arabic word for weight.
A bright star system in the constellation Scorpius, traditionally known as Theta Scorpii.
The brightest star in the constellation Sagittarius, representing the southern part of the Archer's bow.
A bright star in the constellation Carina, formerly part of the historical constellation Argo Navis.
An Arabic name meaning the leader of the daughters of the bier, referring to its position at the end of the Big Dipper handle.
Derived from the Arabic Al-Mankib dhi l-Inan, meaning the shoulder of the rein-holder in the constellation Auriga.
The brightest star in the southern constellation Triangulum Australe, serving as a navigational reference.
A star in the constellation Gemini whose name derives from the Arabic term for the brand on a camel's neck.
Peacock is the brightest star in the southern constellation Pavo, named for the bird associated with the goddess Hera.
Derived from the Arabic Al-Safinah, meaning the ship, representing a star in the constellation Vela which forms part of the mythical Argo Navis.
Derived from the Arabic word for the herald or announcer, as it rises before Sirius.
The current northern pole star in the constellation Ursa Minor, serving as a vital navigational beacon for centuries.
The brightest star in the constellation Hydra, traditionally known as the Solitary One because of its isolation from other bright stars.
The brightest star in the constellation Aries, its name is derived from the Arabic word for head of the sheep.
A star in the constellation Cetus, its name derives from the Arabic term for the second frog.
A star in the handle of the Big Dipper that forms a famous visual binary system with the star Alcor.
A star in the constellation Sagittarius, historically associated with the Babylonian month of the same name.
Derived from the Arabic phrase al-mankib al-qantauris, meaning the shoulder of the centaur, designating the star in the constellation Centaurus.
The brightest star in the constellation Andromeda, traditionally marking the horse's navel in Arabic nomenclature.
A red giant star marking the girdle of the Andromeda constellation.
The name is derived from the Arabic phrase ra's al-hawwa, meaning the head of the serpent charmer.
A multiple star system in the constellation Leo, its name derives from the Arabic term for the forehead of the lion.
A bright star in the constellation Ursa Minor, historically serving as the North Star between 1500 BC and 500 AD.
The name derives from the Arabic term for sword, marking the lower right star in the constellation Orion.
A star in the constellation Leo representing the tail of the lion, derived from the Arabic phrase meaning the lion's tail.
A bright star in the constellation Perseus representing the head of Medusa, known historically as the Demon Star due to its periodic dimming.

A star system in the constellation Centaurus, its name derives from the Arabic term for the two swearers.
A star in the constellation Carina, its name derived from the Greek word for a small shield.
A star in the constellation Vela, its name originates from the Arabic term for the star Canopus, signifying a bright celestial guide.
The brightest star in the constellation Corona Borealis, representing the jewel of the crown.
The rightmost star in Orion's belt, representing the hunter's girdle in Arabic astronomical tradition.
An Arabic name meaning chest, it marks the center of the constellation Cygnus.
The brightest star in the constellation Draco, representing the dragon's head in Arabic astronomical tradition.
The brightest star in the constellation Cassiopeia, representing the queen's breast in Arabic tradition.
The brightest star in the constellation Puppis, its name is derived from the Greek word for a temple or dwelling of a deity.
A bright multiple star system in the constellation Andromeda, derived from the Arabic name for a desert lynx.
A variable star in the constellation Cassiopeia derived from the Arabic word for the palm of a hand.
A binary star system in the constellation Boötes, historically referred to as Pulcherrima or the most beautiful.
An ancient Sumerian term for a mythological creature often translated as mad dog or lion-man, associated with the constellation Lupus.
An Arabic name meaning the forehead of the scorpion, representing the star located in the constellation Scorpius.
A star in the constellation Scorpius known in Philippine astronomy as the herald of the harvest.
A star in the Ursa Major constellation whose name is derived from the Arabic word for loin.
The brightest star in the constellation Phoenix, representing the phoenix rising from its ashes.
The name Girtab is derived from the Sumerian word for scorpion and designates the star Lambda Scorpii in the constellation Scorpius.
The brightest star in the constellation Pegasus, representing the animal's nose.
A red giant star marking the upper-left corner of the Great Square of Pegasus, derived from the Arabic word for the leg.
An Arabic name meaning the preceding one, representing the star Eta Ophiuchi in the constellation of the Serpent Bearer.
A star in the constellation Ursa Major, its name is derived from the Arabic phrase for the thigh of the bear.
A blue supergiant star in the constellation Canis Major, forming part of the figure of the Great Dog.
An Arabic name meaning the right arm, referencing its position within the constellation Cepheus.
An Arabic name meaning the ship, traditionally designating the hull of the constellation Argo Navis.
A star in the Chinese constellation of the Heavenly Stable, representing the official responsible for the celestial horses.
An Arabic name meaning the saddle of the horse, representing the star Alpha Pegasi in the constellation Pegasus.
Derived from the Arabic word for wing, this star marks the right wing of the constellation Cygnus.
A star in the constellation Scorpius, its name is derived from the Arabic word for scorpion.
A blue supergiant star located in the Large Magellanic Cloud, most famous as the progenitor of the supernova SN 1987A.
Named by Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to represent the air pump used by Robert Boyle.
Representing the bird of paradise, this constellation was introduced by Petrus Plancius in the late sixteenth century.
An ancient zodiac constellation representing a water-bearer pouring a stream into the mouth of the Southern Fish.
A constellation representing the eagle that carried Zeus's thunderbolts in Greek mythology.
An ancient southern constellation representing the altar where the gods swore an oath before the war against the Titans.
Representing the golden ram from Greek mythology whose fleece was sought by Jason and the Argonauts.
An ancient constellation representing a charioteer, often associated with the mythological figure Erichthonius of Athens.
An ancient constellation depicted as a herdsman driving the Great Bear around the celestial pole.

A faint constellation representing the crab sent by Hera to hinder Hercules during his battle with the Hydra.

A prominent constellation representing the greater dog following the hunter Orion across the night sky.

A large southern constellation representing a centaur, a creature from Greek mythology with the upper body of a human and the lower body of a horse.
Named after the legendary King of Aethiopia in Greek mythology, husband of Cassiopeia and father of Andromeda.
A large constellation in the southern sky representing the sea monster sent by Poseidon to devour Andromeda.
A faint southern constellation named by Dutch navigators in the late 16th century after the lizard-like reptile.
A faint southern constellation named by Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century after the drafting compass.
A minor constellation introduced in the late 16th century, representing the dove that returned to Noah with an olive branch.
Named for the hair of Queen Berenice II of Egypt, which she sacrificed as a votive offering for her husband's safe return from war.
A small southern constellation representing a crown, often associated with the myths of Sagittarius or Dionysus.
A small constellation representing the crown given by Dionysus to the Cretan princess Ariadne.
A small constellation representing a crow or raven in Greek mythology, associated with the god Apollo.
Representing a cup or goblet in the sky, associated in Greek mythology with the chalice of Apollo.
Small but prominent constellation in the southern sky shaped like a cross and used for navigation.
A prominent northern constellation representing a swan, associated with the Greek myths of Zeus and Leda.
A small northern constellation representing the dolphin that carried the poet Arion to safety in Greek mythology.
A constellation in the southern sky representing the dolphinfish, first introduced by Dutch navigators in the late sixteenth century.

A small northern constellation representing a little horse, cataloged by Ptolemy in the second century.
A sprawling constellation representing the river into which Phaethon fell after losing control of the sun chariot of Helios.

A small prograde irregular moon of Uranus discovered in 2003 and named after a character in William Shakespeare's Much Ado About Nothing.
An irregular moon of Uranus named after the King of Naples in William Shakespeare's play The Tempest.
A small moon of Uranus named after the daughter of Leontes and Hermione in Shakespeare's play The Winter's Tale.
A tiny inner moon of Uranus named after the queen of the fairies in English folklore.
A small inner moon of Uranus, named after the character from Shakespeare's play Timon of Athens.
A moon of Neptune named after the sea nymphs of Greek mythology who were daughters of the sea god Nereus.
Inner moon of Neptune named after the water nymphs of Greek mythology who presided over fountains, wells, and springs.
Named after the primeval Greek goddess of the sea, this moon orbits the planet Neptune.
Named after the daughter of Poseidon and Demeter in Greek mythology, this small moon orbits Neptune.
A moon of Neptune named after the Nereid of Greek mythology who was transformed from a statue into a living woman.
Named after a nymph in Greek mythology who was beloved by Poseidon.
Named for the shape-shifting sea god of Greek mythology, this moon is the second largest of Neptune.
Named after one of the Nereids in Greek mythology, this small moon of Neptune orbits in a highly eccentric and inclined path.
Named after one of the Nereids in Greek mythology, this moon of Neptune is a small, irregular satellite.
A small, irregular moon of Neptune named after a Nereid from Greek mythology.
Named for a Nereid in Greek mythology, this moon orbits the planet Neptune.
Named after one of the Nereids in Greek mythology, this moon of Neptune orbits at an extreme distance from the planet.
A small moon of Neptune named after the sea creature from Greek mythology that is half-horse and half-fish.
Named for an Etruscan underworld deity, this moon orbits the trans-Neptunian object Orcus.
Named for the ferryman in Greek mythology who carried souls across the river Acheron to the underworld.
Named after the Greek personification of the night who preceded the gods of Olympus.
A small moon of Pluto named after the three-headed hound that guards the entrance to the underworld in Greek mythology.
Named for the river of the underworld in Greek mythology, this small moon orbits the dwarf planet Pluto.
The larger moon of the dwarf planet Haumea, named after the Hawaiian goddess born from the creator's mouth.
Named after the Hawaiian goddess of the sea, this moon orbits the dwarf planet Haumea.
Named after a deity from the Tongva people of California, Weywot is the moon of the trans-Neptunian object Quaoar.
Named after the nine-headed venomous serpent of Chinese mythology that brought disaster and floods.
Named for the Greek daemon of lawlessness, this moon orbits the dwarf planet Eris.
